rationale: Recent phases shipped schemas (module_manifest, module_input, module_result, finding, evidence, preview_manifest/1.0, preview_ledger/1.0) and their read-only validators, but no Level 1 module actually produces output that travels the chain end-to-end. The next concrete value is proving the chain works, not extending it.

module_result/1.0 explicitly forbids runtime finding/evidence emission (findings: maxItems 0, evidence: maxItems 0, status enum excludes anything finding-bearing). The candidate direction's phrase 'produce candidate-only finding/evidence fixtures or previews if existing contracts support it' must therefore resolve to the fixtures path; bumping module_result to 1.1 to allow emission is itself a new platform layer and should be rejected here.

preview_manifest/1.0 hard-codes a fixed four-artifact allowlist (run.json, module_inputs.json, module_results.json, bundle_consistency.json). Adding a new artifact for candidate findings would require preview_manifest/1.1 plus a parallel validator change — another platform-layer expansion P2-16 must avoid.

preview_ledger/1.0 is a binding index of preview manifests, not a findings index; expanding it now would compound the over-engineering risk before any module has actually emitted into the preview path.

Workflow validation is best served by a thin, pure-function Level 1 check whose committed golden outputs prove the contracts are coherent, plus committed triage drafts that prove the human/agent review step is real. That validates the chain without touching any 1.0 contract.

recommended_scope.workflow_goal: Validate the end-to-end Level 1 workflow chain (committed input fixture -> deterministic offline check function -> committed candidate-finding golden fixture conforming to finding/1.0 -> committed Claude/Cowork triage markdown draft) for a single low-risk, well-understood header-baseline check, without bumping any 1.0 contract, without runtime finding emission, and without any network or target-touching surface. The deliverable is proof that the contracts and review steps line up coherently, not a new generic platform layer.
allowed_inputs: Committed offline header fixtures at tests/fixtures/security_headers_baseline/<scenario>/input.json, each conforming to a small closed JSON shape documented in the module README (fixture_version literal, target.type in {url, domain}, target.value as a placeholder like 'lab.local' or 'example.test', status_code int, headers as a closed list of {name, value} pairs).
A required --run-id CLI argument matching the existing safe-run-id regex used by module_runner.

A required --policy-decision-sha256 CLI argument (64 lowercase hex) supplied by the caller so emitted candidate findings satisfy finding/1.0.source.policy_decision_sha256 without the module synthesizing it.
Read-only access to the per-header check registry committed inside the module (header_slug -> rule_id -> severity_hint, fixed lookup table, no computed scoring).
forbidden_behavior: No imports of socket, urllib, urllib2, urllib3, http, http.client, httplib, ssl, ftplib, smtplib, telnetlib, xmlrpc, asyncio, subprocess, threading, multiprocessing, selectors, asyncore, requests, httpx, aiohttp.
No DNS resolution, no name resolution, no environment variable reads beyond a small explicit allowlist (none required for v1; reads should be zero).

No filesystem writes anywhere in the runtime path: no open(..., 'w'), no Path.write_text/write_bytes, no os.makedirs/os.mkdir/os.rename/os.unlink/os.remove, no shutil copy/move/rmtree. CLI is stdout-only.

No runtime emission into module_result.json, preview bundle artifacts, runs/, loot/, evidence/, reports/, config/scope.txt, or .hermes.md. v1 emits to stdout only and is reconciled against committed golden fixtures in tests.
No wiring into module_runner write-path, recon.sh, schedulers, CI, pre-commit, hooks, or any external publishing surface in P2-16.
No new schema files in modules/_schema/, no bump to any 1.0 contract, no new artifact in preview_manifest's allowlist, no new entry shape in preview_ledger, no new validator script.

No acceptance of url/domain/ip/cidr targets from CLI beyond what the committed fixture's placeholder target.value already contains; explicit --target/--url/--host flags are not implemented and not accepted.
No Mozilla-Observatory-style numeric scoring, no aggregate grade, no severity computation; severity_hint per emitted finding comes from a fixed per-rule lookup table only.
No echoing of raw header VALUES into finding.title/summary/remediation/verification_guidance; values may not appear in v1 outputs at all (evidence ref array stays empty in v1).
No live live-mode toggle, no opt-in network flag, no 'just for testing' egress path; the module's network surface is permanently zero in v1.

oss_recon_gate.source: OWASP Secure Headers Project (header categories and recommended values for CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, Permissions-Policy)
oss_recon_gate.rationale: Adopt the header taxonomy and the recommended-value guidance as the authoritative reference for the per-rule lookup table. Do not adopt their server-side scanner code, any HTTP fetching, or any live deployment behavior. The module's per-header rule set should be a small, hand-curated subset (5 headers in v1) drawn from this taxonomy, not a wholesale import.

oss_recon_gate.source: OWASP ASVS v4 V14.4 HTTP Security Headers requirements
oss_recon_gate.rationale: Use ASVS V14.4 wording as the canonical reference for each rule's verification_guidance text. Do not adopt the broader ASVS taxonomy (V1-V13, V15+) or any ASVS-level assessment scaffolding; v1 is one narrow check, not an ASVS verification harness.

oss_recon_gate.source: OWASP ZAP passive scanner header rules (alert IDs for missing/weak headers)
oss_recon_gate.rationale: Borrow the per-header rule IDEAS (presence, absence, weak-value categories) and use them as inspiration for the local rule_id namespace (security_headers_baseline.<header_slug>.<rule>). Do not import ZAP code, ZAP alert IDs verbatim, or any ZAP active-scan / live-spidering behavior. ZAP integration as a runtime is explicitly out of scope.

oss_recon_gate.source: Mozilla Observatory (mozilla/http-observatory) numeric scoring and live HTTP scanning
oss_recon_gate.rationale: Observatory performs live HTTPS requests and computes a numeric grade. Both are out of scope and would normalize target-touching defaults and scoring algorithms the project has explicitly deferred. Do not adopt the grading model, the scoring weights, or the scan loop.

Hosted live scanner against external targets; adopting any of its behavior risks blurring the offline-only boundary and implicitly endorsing third-party target-touching tooling. Reference only as a comparison point in the module README if at all.

finding/1.0 and evidence/1.0 already define the project's candidate-output shape. Pulling in SARIF result/run semantics would create a parallel shape and pressure to add a SARIF importer/exporter — exactly the platform-layer expansion P2-16 is meant to pause.
Tightly coupled to a remote service, engagement/test lifecycle, and finding ingestion. Any adoption implies an upload or tracking surface, which violates the offline/data-only boundary.
Template format is driven by live scanning, request templating, and severity heuristics. Embedding it normalizes target-touching defaults and would pull in a templating engine v1 has no use for.

implementation_requirements: Place the module at modules/checks/level1/security_headers_baseline/check.py alongside the existing module.json pattern; if a module.json is added, set requires_network=false, network_access='none', target_touching=false, intrusive=false, destructive=false, and emits_findings=false (true emission lands later in a phase with its own T3 review).
Stdlib-only: json, re, hashlib, pathlib, datetime, argparse, dataclasses, sys, typing. No third-party deps. No optional fall-through imports (no try/except ImportError of forbidden modules).

Pure-function core: evaluate(fixture: dict, *, run_id: str, policy_decision_sha256: str) -> list[dict] returning a list of finding/1.0-shaped dicts (status='candidate', triage.scanner_output_only=true, triage.manual_verification_required=true). Deterministic over input; same input -> byte-identical canonical JSON output every run.

Closed input fixture shape, validated at the top of evaluate(): required keys exactly {fixture_version, target, status_code, headers}; fixture_version is the exact literal 'security_headers_baseline_input/1'; target is {type in {'url','domain'}, value: string <=500 chars}; status_code is int 100..599; headers is a list of {name: string matching ^[A-Za-z0-9!#$%&'*+\-.^_`|~]{1,80}$, value: string <=4096 chars}. Unknown top-level or nested keys are a hard reject (FixtureShapeError) with field path.

Per-rule lookup table committed inline in check.py: header_slug -> rule_id -> {severity_hint, title_template, summary_template, remediation_template, verification_guidance_template, owasp_ref, cwe_ref}. Templates render with header NAME only (no header VALUE), the rule_id, and the static OWASP/CWE references; no value interpolation, no host/IP/URL interpolation.

v1 header coverage set is exactly five headers: Content-Security-Policy (presence + 'unsafe-inline' presence as separate rule), X-Frame-Options (presence + DENY/SAMEORIGIN), X-Content-Type-Options (presence + nosniff literal), Strict-Transport-Security (presence + max-age>=15552000 + includeSubDomains literal), Referrer-Policy (presence + value in a fixed allowlist). Permissions-Policy, COOP/COEP/CORP, NEL, Report-To, Cache-Control, and Set-Cookie flag checks are deferred.

Emitted candidate findings populate source.module_id='level1.security_headers_baseline', source.run_id from the CLI argument, source.policy_decision_sha256 from the CLI argument; the module does not synthesize a run_id or a policy_decision_sha256 (no implicit defaults).

Emitted candidate findings have evidence=[] in v1 (finding/1.0 allows an empty array). Evidence emission is deferred to a future phase that justifies the runs/<run_id>/evidence/ write surface explicitly.

CLI: python check.py --fixture <path> --run-id <run_id> --policy-decision-sha256 <64-hex>; missing/invalid args fail-closed with nonzero exit and a typed error to stderr. On success, prints a single-line JSON array of candidate findings to stdout and exits 0. No stderr noise on success.
Static no-network test: assert the source of check.py does not contain any of the forbidden import names listed under forbidden_behavior.
Static no-write test: assert the source does not contain any of the forbidden write call patterns listed under forbidden_behavior.
Redaction-safe output: assert post-render that no emitted finding string field contains the literal target.value or any header VALUE from the input fixture; only header NAMES and rule IDs may appear.

Each emitted candidate finding is validated against modules/_schema/finding.schema.json using a stdlib-only walker (since the project has no jsonschema dep). The validation step is internal to the test suite, not a runtime gate.
No integration into module_runner, no entry in modules/profiles/*, no new validator script, no preview bundle generation, no preview_ledger entry, no contract bump.
test_requirements: Commit 6 input fixtures under tests/fixtures/security_headers_baseline/<scenario>/input.json covering: all-headers-present (clean), all-headers-absent, weak-csp (header present with 'unsafe-inline'), weak-hsts (max-age too low, no includeSubDomains), partial-set (3 of 5 headers present), non-200-status (e.g., 301 redirect, to confirm rules apply uniformly).

Commit 6 matching golden candidate-finding fixtures under tests/fixtures/security_headers_baseline/<scenario>/expected_findings.json. Each is the canonical-JSON output of evaluate() on the matching input.
Test: for every scenario, evaluate(input, run_id='p2-16.fixture', policy_decision_sha256='<64-hex committed in the fixture>') produces canonical-JSON output equal to expected_findings.json.
Test: each finding in expected_findings.json validates against modules/_schema/finding.schema.json (stdlib walker).
Test: each finding's status='candidate', triage.scanner_output_only=true, triage.manual_verification_required=true.
Test: each finding's source.module_id='level1.security_headers_baseline', source.run_id matches the committed value, source.policy_decision_sha256 matches the committed value.

Test: each finding's title, summary, remediation, verification_guidance fields contain no host, IP, URL, header value, or environment value (assert via a redaction regex pass over the rendered strings).
Test: missing required input key (each of fixture_version, target, status_code, headers in turn) -> FixtureShapeError with the missing field path.
Test: unknown extra top-level key in input fixture -> FixtureShapeError (closed shape).
Test: fixture_version drift ('security_headers_baseline_input/1.0', '/2', missing, non-string) -> FixtureShapeError.
Test: header name not matching the safe-header-name regex -> FixtureShapeError.
Test: determinism: evaluate() called twice on the same input produces byte-identical canonical JSON.
Test: pure-function guarantee: snapshot mtimes/sizes/contents of tests/fixtures/security_headers_baseline/ before and after the full test suite; no changes.
Test: static import test asserts check.py does not import any of the forbidden net/IPC modules listed in forbidden_behavior.
Test: static no-write test asserts check.py does not call any of the forbidden write functions listed in forbidden_behavior.

Test: CLI smoke (subprocess.run is acceptable in the TEST file, not in the module) — missing --run-id -> nonzero exit; invalid --policy-decision-sha256 (not 64 hex) -> nonzero exit; valid run -> stdout is a single-line JSON array, stderr empty, exit 0.

Test: header name and value matching are case-insensitive for name (per RFC 7230) but verbatim for value comparisons in CSP/HSTS sub-token checks; assert both behaviors explicitly with a fixture pair.

Test: no fixture's target.value resolves to a real address — committed values are restricted to 'lab.local', '*.example.test', 'invalid.', or RFC 2606 reserved names; assert this with a regex over all committed input fixtures.
triage_review_requirements: For each of the 6 committed scenarios, commit a Claude/Cowork triage draft at handoff/p2_16_triage/<scenario>.md. The draft cites each candidate finding by rule_id, names the header by NAME only, references the OWASP Secure Headers Project section and ASVS V14.4 requirement number, and proposes a 2-4 sentence remediation that a human reviewer can accept verbatim into a report.

Triage drafts MUST NOT contain any host name, IP address, URL, header value, environment value, or any string that appears in the input fixture's headers[*].value or target.value field. Drafts may name the rule_id, header name, severity_hint, and the OWASP/ASVS/CWE references.
Triage drafts are markdown only — they are not findings, not evidence, not reports, not published artifacts. They live in handoff/ where the rest of the agent-review traffic lives.

The workflow validation passes when, for every committed scenario, all three of (input fixture, golden candidate-finding JSON, triage markdown draft) exist in git and are mutually consistent (rule_ids in the triage draft match rule_ids in the golden JSON, which match rule_ids in evaluate()'s output).
Triage authoring is performed without touching the network, scope, runs/, loot/, evidence/, reports/, or any production setting. No git push, no PR open from the triage step.
Add a one-page handoff/p2_16_triage/README.md documenting the triage cadence (draft -> human-reviewer approval -> only then any report draft) so future agents do not collapse the review step.

blocking_concerns.summary: The candidate direction's phrase 'produce candidate-only finding/evidence fixtures or previews if existing contracts support it' is ambiguous. module_result/1.0 explicitly forbids findings (maxItems: 0) and evidence (maxItems: 0), and its status enum has no finding-bearing value. preview_manifest/1.0 hard-codes a four-artifact allowlist that does not include a findings artifact. Therefore live runtime emission via the existing chain is not possible without a contract bump, and the strategy review's 'pause platform layers' guidance argues against that bump.
blocking_concerns.required_change: Lock the P2-16 sprint to the fixtures path: the module is a pure function whose committed golden outputs ARE the workflow-validation artifact. Defer runtime emission (and any module_result/1.1 or preview_manifest/1.1 bump that would enable it) to a future T3 phase that justifies the new write surface explicitly. The implementation review must reject any PR that bumps a 1.0 contract under cover of P2-16.

blocking_concerns.summary: 'Committed sanitized HTTP response/header fixtures' is too loose. A free-form .http or raw HTTP wire-format file invites future drift toward live HTTP parsing, header normalization corner cases, and eventually a real HTTP client.
blocking_concerns.required_change: Pin the input fixture as a closed JSON document with the literal fixture_version 'security_headers_baseline_input/1' and the exact key set {fixture_version, target, status_code, headers}; reject unknown keys with a FixtureShapeError. Document the shape in modules/checks/level1/security_headers_baseline/README.md. No separate schema file in modules/_schema/ (that would be the platform-layer expansion this phase is meant to avoid).

blocking_concerns.summary: The check function and CLI must not accept a live target (url/domain/ip/cidr) even though module.json's target_types field permits them at the manifest level. Any --target/--url/--host flag, even unimplemented but reserved, creates a slippery slope toward live-mode toggles.
blocking_concerns.required_change: v1 CLI accepts only --fixture <path>, --run-id <id>, --policy-decision-sha256 <hex>. No --target/--url/--host/--scope/--live flag exists. Committed input fixtures use only RFC 2606 reserved names ('lab.local', '*.example.test', 'invalid.') as target.value; add a test that asserts this over the entire tests/fixtures/security_headers_baseline/ tree.

blocking_concerns.summary: Mozilla Observatory and SecurityHeaders.com both compute numeric grades. Adopting any scoring would import live-scanner DNA the project has explicitly pushed away from, and would invite future 'why does our grade differ from theirs?' pressure.
blocking_concerns.required_change: severity_hint per rule is a fixed lookup committed inline in check.py. No numeric score, no aggregate grade, no computed weight. Module README must explicitly state that severity assignment is per-rule static and that any future scoring must go through its own T3 review.

blocking_concerns.summary: Any os.makedirs, open(..., 'w'), Path.write_text/write_bytes, or shutil copy/move/rmtree call in check.py creates a new persistence surface and pressures the project to define a new artifact layout — exactly the kind of platform-layer growth that should be paused.
blocking_concerns.required_change: check.py performs zero filesystem writes at runtime. CLI prints to stdout only. Tests assert this statically by scanning the source for forbidden write call patterns. If a future phase needs disk output, it justifies the write surface in its own T3 review.

non_blocking_recommendations: Keep v1 to exactly the 5 headers listed in implementation_requirements; defer Permissions-Policy, COOP/COEP/CORP, NEL, Report-To, Cache-Control, and Set-Cookie flag checks to a future phase. Resist the temptation to ship 'while we are in here' because that is the exact failure mode the strategy review flagged.

Commit a short modules/checks/level1/security_headers_baseline/README.md documenting (a) the closed input fixture shape, (b) the per-rule lookup table, (c) the redaction guarantee, (d) the no-network/no-write boundary, and (e) the OWASP Secure Headers Project and ASVS V14.4 references. Do not reference Mozilla Observatory or SecurityHeaders.com in the README to avoid normalizing live-scanner inspiration in future contributors' minds.

Name rule IDs as security_headers_baseline.<header_slug>.<rule> (e.g., security_headers_baseline.csp.missing, security_headers_baseline.csp.unsafe_inline, security_headers_baseline.hsts.max_age_too_low). This keeps future header additions namespaced and grep-friendly without requiring a registry file.
Use canonical JSON output (sorted keys, no trailing whitespace, ensure_ascii=False) for both stdout and the committed expected_findings.json files so diffs in CI are minimal and reviewable.

When defining the per-rule remediation_template strings, draft them as if they will be quoted verbatim into a human-authored report; this <program-name>-loads the triage step's review and reduces churn.

Add a one-line entry to modules/_schema/README.md (or a sibling doc) noting that P2-16 deliberately did NOT bump any 1.0 contract and that future runtime-emission work must be reviewed as its own T3 phase. This protects the boundary for the next agent.

Reserve (but do not implement in P2-16) the naming for a future evidence emission path: runs/<run_id>/evidence/security_headers_baseline/<rule_id>/<sha256>.json. Document the deferral so the future phase does not collide with another module's evidence layout.

Reserve (but do not implement) a future module_result/1.1 that relaxes findings to maxItems > 0 only when emits_findings=true in module.json, gated behind a per-module manifest flag. Document the deferral; do not write the schema now.

Consider committing a tiny CHANGELOG.md fragment for the module that records 'v0.1.0 — workflow validation, fixture-driven, no runtime emission' so the intentional scoping is preserved across future contributors.

If a future phase wants to wire this module into module_runner's dry-run path, that work must add explicit tests that the module continues to perform zero writes and zero network calls even when invoked under module_runner. Capture this as a deferred requirement so it is not forgotten.
explicit_non_goals: No new schema files in modules/_schema/.
No bump to module_manifest/1.0, module_input/1.0, module_result/1.0, run/1.0, finding/1.0, evidence/1.0, preview_manifest/1.0, or preview_ledger/1.0.
No new artifact added to the preview_manifest/1.0 four-artifact allowlist.
No new entry in preview_ledger and no preview_ledger builder/indexer.
No HTTP requests, no DNS, no socket, no IPC, no subprocess, no threads, no async, no SSL/TLS code path, no third-party deps.
No Mozilla-Observatory-style numeric scoring, no aggregate grade, no computed severity, no scan loop.
No live targets, no live-mode toggle, no --target/--url/--host CLI flag, no scope expansion, no edit to config/scope.txt or .hermes.md.
No runtime emission of findings or evidence into runs/, loot/, evidence/, reports/, or anywhere on disk.
No wiring into module_runner write-path, recon.sh, schedulers, CI workflows, pre-commit hooks, post-commit hooks, or deployment.
No automated triage that publishes anything; triage drafts are markdown reviewed by humans and never auto-promoted.
No agent-assisted live HTTP fetching 'just to populate fixtures'; all fixtures are hand-authored against RFC 2606 reserved names.
No SARIF, DefectDojo, ZAP, or Nuclei integration code in P2-16 (references in documentation are fine; runtime adoption is not).
No new validator script; existing preview_manifest and preview_ledger validators are not modified.
No git push, no PR open, no remote-tracking field, no notification surface, no remote_id, no upload semantics.
No expansion to a second module, second profile, or second header set in P2-16 — one module, five headers, six fixtures, six triage drafts, then stop.
